sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Matthew, has been experiencing lower back pain for a while, which worsens with standing, walking, and activities such as coughing and sneezing.
The pain is described as an electrical toothache and radiates down the left leg, affecting the hip, side of the calf, and big toe.
The patient rates the pain as a 6 out of 10 at the moment, but it can reach up to 13 out of 10 when severe.
The pain is exacerbated by standing and walking, but laying down provides some relief.
The patient has been experiencing numbness and tingling in both legs, with weakness in both the back and legs.
The patient has been unable to perform daily activities due to the pain, including tying shoelaces and walking without a cane.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of a fall 30 years ago that may have resulted in a possible fracture, but the exact diagnosis is unknown.
The patient has been bedridden for a while due to the pain.
Review of systems: The patient's pain affects the lower back, hip, side of the calf, and big toe, with radiation down the left leg.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient has positive pain to palpation at the L4 vertebrae, with pain with flexion and extension of the back.
The patient has 4 out of 5 strength in the left leg and 5 out of 5 strength in the right leg.
Reflexes are brisk and motor and sensory are intact in both lower extremities.
Test Results: The MRI shows a disk herniation at the level of L4-L5 vertebrae with associated nerve root impingement.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has acute disk herniation at L4-L5 with nerve root impingement causing lower back pain and pain radiating down the left leg.
Problem list: Acute disk herniation at L4-L5 with nerve root impingement; lower back pain; pain radiating down the left leg; numbness and tingling in both legs; weakness in both the back and legs.
Plan:
I recommended an epidural steroid injection to place anti-inflammatory medication at the site of inflammation.
I will refer the patient to physical therapy (PT) to strengthen the area.
The patient will see me again in about a month to assess the effectiveness of the injection.
Follow-up: The patient will see me again in about a month.
